facts.title: Hangul was invented, not evolved
facts.fact: Korea's alphabet, Hangul, was created in 1443 under King Sejong the Great. It is one of the few writing systems with a known inventor and creation date, and its letter shapes mirror the mouth and tongue positions used to make each sound.
facts.korean_en: King Sejong created Hangul.
facts.note: **한글날** (Hangul Day) is a national holiday on **October 9** that celebrates the alphabet.

facts.title: The Three Kingdoms
facts.fact: For centuries the peninsula was divided among three kingdoms — Goguryeo, Baekje, and Silla — until Silla unified most of it in 668.
facts.korean_en: Long ago there were three kingdoms in Korea.
facts.note: 고구려, 백제, 신라 are the **삼국** (Three Kingdoms).

facts.title: Seoul, a 600-year capital
facts.fact: When the Joseon dynasty made Seoul its capital in 1394, it began a run of more than 600 years as Korea's center of power that continues today.
facts.korean_en: Seoul is the capital of Korea.
facts.note: 한국의 means "Korea's" — the particle **의** marks possession.

facts.title: A mountainous country
facts.fact: About 70% of South Korea is mountainous, so cities cluster in valleys and along the coast. Hiking is one of the most popular weekend activities.
facts.korean_en: There are many mountains in Korea.
facts.note: 주말에 등산을 해요 = "I hike on the weekend." 등산 literally means "climb-mountain."

facts.title: Jeju, the volcanic island
facts.fact: Jeju Island off the south coast is volcanic and home to Hallasan, South Korea's highest peak at 1,947 m. It is a favorite honeymoon and holiday destination.
facts.korean_en: Jeju Island is a beautiful island.
facts.note: In **제주도**, the 도 means "island/province."

facts.title: The Han River through Seoul
facts.fact: The Han River runs through the middle of Seoul. Its riverside parks are popular for picnics, bike rides, and ordering fried chicken by delivery.
facts.korean_en: I ride a bicycle at the Han River.
facts.note: **치맥** (치킨 + 맥주, "chicken + beer") by the river is a beloved tradition.

facts.title: Four distinct seasons
facts.fact: Korea has four clear seasons: spring with cherry blossoms, hot humid summer, crisp colorful autumn, and a cold dry winter with snow.
facts.korean_en: Korea has four seasons.
facts.note: **사계절** = "four seasons." 벚꽃 (cherry blossom) season in spring is a big event.

facts.title: A divided peninsula
facts.fact: Since 1948 the peninsula has been divided into the Republic of Korea (South) and the DPRK (North), separated by the DMZ near the 38th parallel.
facts.korean_en: Korea is divided into south and north.
facts.note: South Korea's official name is **대한민국**; the DMZ truce line is the 휴전선.

facts.title: A young, lively democracy
facts.fact: South Korea is a democratic republic with a directly elected president. Its democracy is young — direct presidential elections returned only in 1987 — and turnout is famously high.
facts.korean_en: Korean people vote.
facts.note: 투표를 하다 = "to vote" (literally "to do a vote"). Many nouns pair with **하다** to form verbs.

facts.title: A Nobel for Korean literature
facts.fact: In 2024, novelist Han Kang became the first Korean to win the Nobel Prize in Literature, recognized for prose that confronts historical trauma.
facts.korean_en: The author Han Kang won the Nobel Prize in Literature.
facts.note: A nice coincidence: the author **한강** is written exactly like the **한강** (Han River).

facts.title: The sijo, a three-line poem
facts.fact: The sijo is a traditional three-line Korean poem dating back to the Goryeo dynasty — short and structured, comparable in spirit to the Japanese haiku.
facts.korean_en: Sijo is a traditional Korean poem.
facts.note: A **시조** has three lines of about 14–16 syllables each.

facts.title: Kimchi at every meal
facts.fact: Kimchi — fermented vegetables seasoned with chili and garlic — accompanies almost every Korean meal. Families traditionally make big batches in late autumn in an event called gimjang.
facts.korean_en: I like kimchi.
facts.note: **김장** (gimjang), the communal kimchi-making, is on UNESCO's intangible heritage list.

facts.title: Rice, soup, and many side dishes
facts.fact: A Korean meal centers on rice and soup with many small shared side dishes called banchan, which are refilled for free at most restaurants.
facts.korean_en: I eat rice and side dishes.
facts.note: **하고** means "and" when joining nouns, and is very common in speech.

facts.title: Grill-it-yourself barbecue
facts.fact: At a Korean barbecue you grill meat at your own table and wrap it in lettuce with garlic, ssamjang, and rice. Meals are social and shared.
facts.korean_en: I eat meat with friends.
facts.note: **삼겹살** (samgyeopsal, pork belly) is the most popular cut.

facts.title: Speech changes with respect
facts.fact: Korean has several speech levels. The same idea changes form depending on who you address — the verb 먹다 (to eat) becomes polite 먹어요 or honorific 드세요.
facts.korean_en: Please eat slowly. (said warmly to a guest)
facts.note: **존댓말** is polite/honorific speech; **반말** is casual speech used with close friends.

facts.title: Two number systems
facts.fact: Korean uses two sets of numbers: native Korean (하나, 둘, 셋…) for counting things and ages, and Sino-Korean (일, 이, 삼…) for dates, money, and minutes.
facts.korean_en: I bought three apples.
facts.note: 세 개 uses native 셋 → 세 with the counter **개**. Money uses Sino-Korean: 삼천 원 = 3,000 won.

facts.title: Seollal, Lunar New Year
facts.fact: Seollal, the Lunar New Year, is one of Korea's biggest holidays. Families gather, hold ancestral rites, and children bow to elders to receive money and blessings.
facts.korean_en: I meet my family on Lunar New Year.
facts.note: On 설날 people eat **떡국** (rice-cake soup); eating it is playfully said to add a year to your age.

facts.title: Chuseok, the harvest festival
facts.fact: Chuseok, the autumn harvest festival, is often called "Korean Thanksgiving." People travel to their hometowns and share songpyeon, half-moon rice cakes.
facts.korean_en: Chuseok is an autumn holiday.
facts.note: **명절** is a traditional holiday, as opposed to 휴일, an ordinary day off.

facts.title: The Korean Wave
facts.fact: The "Korean Wave" has carried K-pop, dramas, and film around the world. In 2020, Bong Joon-ho's "Parasite" became the first non-English film to win the Best Picture Oscar.
facts.korean_en: I listen to K-pop often.
facts.note: **한류** (Hallyu) literally means "Korean flow/wave."

facts.title: Korea invented the webtoon
facts.fact: Korea pioneered the webtoon — vertical-scroll digital comics made for phones. Many hit dramas and films are now adapted from popular webtoons.
facts.korean_en: I read webtoons every day.
facts.note: **웹툰** = "web" + 툰 (from "cartoon").

facts.title: Traditional markets
facts.fact: Traditional markets such as Seoul's Namdaemun and Gwangjang sell everything from clothes to street food. Friendly haggling is common at market stalls, though not in shops.
facts.korean_en: I buy fruit at the market.
facts.note: **깎아 주세요** = "Please give me a discount" (literally "please cut [the price]").

facts.title: Convenience stores do everything
facts.fact: Convenience stores are everywhere and offer far more than snacks — hot meals, package pickup, even simple banking. Online orders often arrive the very next morning.
facts.korean_en: I buy gimbap at the convenience store.
facts.note: **배달** (delivery) culture is huge — almost anything can be delivered to your door.

facts.title: The won, and a familiar face
facts.fact: The currency is the won. Coins run from 10 to 500 won and bills from 1,000 to 50,000 won. The 10,000-won note features King Sejong, who created Hangul.
facts.korean_en: How much is this?
facts.note: **얼마예요?** is the essential shopping question.

facts.title: Gyeongbokgung Palace
facts.fact: Gyeongbokgung, built in 1395, is Seoul's grand main palace. Visitors who wear hanbok, traditional dress, get in free.
facts.korean_en: Gyeongbokgung is very big.
facts.note: Catch the changing-of-the-guard ceremony (**수문장 교대식**) at the main gate.

facts.title: Bukchon Hanok Village
facts.fact: Bukchon Hanok Village preserves hundreds of traditional tile-roof houses on hilly lanes between two palaces — a favorite spot for photos in hanbok.
facts.korean_en: A hanok is a traditional Korean house.
facts.note: A **한옥** uses under-floor heating called 온돌 to stay warm in winter.

facts.title: Busan by the sea
facts.fact: Busan, the second-largest city, is a coastal hub famous for Haeundae Beach, the huge Jagalchi fish market, and a major international film festival.
facts.korean_en: Busan has a beautiful sea.
facts.note: The KTX train from Seoul to **부산** takes about two and a half hours.

facts.title: Wired and fast
facts.fact: South Korea has some of the world's fastest average internet speeds and near-universal smartphone use. Transit, payments, and even restaurant orders are heavily app-based.
facts.korean_en: Korea's internet is fast.
facts.note: **와이파이** (wifi) is widely available in cafés, subways, and buses.

facts.title: High-speed rail
facts.fact: The KTX high-speed train links major cities at up to 300 km/h, putting most of the country within a few hours of Seoul.
facts.korean_en: I go to Busan by train.
facts.note: In 기차로, the particle **(으)로** marks the means: "by train."

facts.title: Shoes off indoors
facts.fact: You remove your shoes before entering a Korean home, and often at traditional restaurants with floor seating. Because floors are heated, sitting on the floor is comfortable.
facts.korean_en: I take off my shoes in the house.
facts.note: **온돌** (ondol) is the traditional under-floor heating system.

facts.title: Age shapes everything
facts.fact: Age shapes language and relationships, so people often ask your age early on. In 2023 Korea officially switched to international age counting for legal use, retiring the older "Korean age."
facts.korean_en: How old are you? (polite)
facts.note: A more casual version is **몇 살이에요?** Use the polite form with people you have just met.
